summary: Gruber poses five pointed questions to Google after their announcement that Chrome would drop H.264 video codec support in favor of open codecs like WebM. He highlights the apparent hypocrisy of Google citing 'open innovation' while continuing to bundle Adobe's proprietary Flash Player in Chrome. He extends the challenge to Android's H.264 support and YouTube's H.264 encoding, questioning whether Google will apply the same open-standards logic consistently across its own products. The post implies Google's move is strategically motivated rather than principled.
key_points: Google's stated rationale of 'open innovation' for dropping H.264 is inconsistent with Chrome's continued bundling of Adobe's proprietary Flash Player
Google's own products—Android and YouTube—rely heavily on H.264, raising questions about whether the same standard will be applied there
Major video streaming services use H.264, and without it Chrome users would ironically need Flash (proprietary) to watch H.264 content
The final question—'Who is happy about this?'—frames the decision as benefiting no one except Google's strategic interests
money_quotes: If H.264 support is being removed to 'enable open innovation', will Flash Player support be dropped as well? If not, why?
YouTube uses H.264 to encode video. Presumably, YouTube will be re-encoding its entire library using WebM.
Do you expect companies like Netflix, Amazon, Vimeo, Major League Baseball, and anyone else who currently streams H.264 to dual-encode all of their video using WebM?
How will Chrome users watch this content other than by resorting to Flash Player's support for H.264 playback?
key_insight: Google's removal of H.264 from Chrome in the name of openness is undermined by its simultaneous embrace of proprietary Flash and its own products' dependence on H.264.
